articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects broader demand erosion, procedural delays, or temporary policy anxiety across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time visa processing data, OPT policy developments, and competitor messaging from the UK, Australia, and New Zealand, while preparing transparent, evidence-based guidance for prospective students on post-study work pathways and application timelines.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been confirmed, and the government’s international education strategy emphasizes managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 visa reforms—though still undefined in timing and detail—may modestly improve its competitiveness for international students seeking post-study work, particularly from Asia and the Pacific, though demand shifts will depend on implementation fidelity and comparative policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should track New Zealand’s official guidance as late-2026 approaches, benchmark these proposals against evolving UK, Australia, and Canada post-study work rules, and assess whether to adjust messaging for students prioritising graduate employment pathways.

articles.title: British Council offers masters scholarships in the UK exclusively for women
articles.summary: The British Council has opened applications for full master’s scholarships exclusively for women in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). These scholarships are offered through the Women in STEM program and provide places at the University of Warwick and Aston University in England.
articles.insight:
This initiative signals growing institutional emphasis on gender equity in STEM recruitment, potentially influencing international students’ perceptions of inclusivity and opportunity—though its direct impact on overall demand remains uncertain without data on uptake or spillover effects across other destinations.  
Admissions teams should monitor whether similar targeted scholarships emerge in their regions and assess if prospective women in STEM applicants increasingly prioritize equity-focused support, while reviewing current outreach to ensure alignment with evolving diversity expectations.

articles.title: Dozens of international students who won Chevening scholarships were denied visas
articles.summary: The UK Home Office denied visa applications for dozens of international students who had been awarded Chevening scholarships from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan. This decision followed a prior ban on student visas for nationals from these countries.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential volatility in UK student visa policy for nationals from high-risk or conflict-affected regions, possibly dampening demand for UK study among students from Afghanistan, Cameroon, Myanmar, and Sudan—though broader implications for global recruitment remain uncertain pending further data on appeal outcomes and policy consistency.  
Admissions teams should monitor visa refusal trends and sentiment shifts in these source markets via agent feedback and social listening, while reviewing contingency plans for students whose UK applications face unexpected barriers—particularly those from Southeast Asia and West Africa with overlapping nationality or residency ties.

articles.title: UK denies visas to some winners of Chevening, its own most prestigious scholarship
articles.summary: The UK government has denied student visas to dozens of Chevening Scholarship winners, its most prestigious international scholarship program. This occurred after the UK imposed a blanket ban on student visas from four countries affected by war.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential reputational and trust risks for the UK’s international education brand, possibly dampening demand from conflict-affected regions—even if broader visa policy shifts remain uncertain and data on actual enrolment impacts is still emerging.  
Admissions teams should monitor visa refusal trends by nationality, track student sentiment in high-risk source markets via surveys or agent feedback, and review contingency messaging around visa reliability—especially when competing with countries promoting stable, predictable pathways.

articles.title: UK ELT reports challenging enrolment trends continued through last quarter of 2025
articles.summary: English UK’s Q4 2025 Quarterly Intelligence Cohort (QUIC) data shows the UK ELT sector continued to face challenging enrolment trends throughout the year. While there was a year-on-year increase in enrolments compared to Q4 2024, overall volumes remained significantly below pre-COVID levels.
articles.insight:
The UK’s persistently low ELT enrolments—despite a modest YoY Q4 2025 uptick—may reflect broader shifts in international student preferences, tighter visa compliance, or reduced confidence in post-study pathways, though causality remains unclear without granular source-market or policy data.  
Admissions teams should track whether declining ELT participation correlates with falling undergraduate/postgraduate applications from key markets like India or Nigeria, and assess if competitors are adjusting scholarship offers or pathway partnerships to capture displaced demand.
